narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam. The model assesses the likelihood of passing the bar on the first try based on academic records, test scores, and personal circumstances like yours. For you specifically, it predicted a 52% probability of failing the bar exam, which tipped the scales toward rejecting your application to prioritize admitting students with stronger projected odds of success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish level, signaling to the model that your later academic performance might not hold up under bar exam pressures. This stood out as the top driver, more than any other aspect of your profile. On a more positive note, your middle family income actually nudged the prediction toward passing, as it aligns with many successful bar takers who had similar support levels. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.5, also below the average of around 3.2, provided some counterbalance in favor of passing but wasn't strong enough to offset the concerns. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2 offered a slight positive pull, your full-time study plans helped a bit since most students pursue that path successfully, but your LSAT score of 25—much lower than the usual 37—added a modest push toward the failure side. Compared to typical applicants, your grades and LSAT placed you in the lower deciles and percentiles, which is why the model saw higher risk in your case despite the everyday elements like your family income and full-time status. I know this rejection stings, especially when you've worked hard to get here, but it's not a judgment on your potential—it's just the model's data-driven read. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep programs, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and growth. With targeted improvements in those key areas, you could reapply stronger and shift the odds in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you've got this.
